flashcards.front: Why are rules on customs valuation needed?
flashcards.back: They are needed to ensure a fair, uniform, and neutral system for valuing goods for customs purposes and to prevent arbitrary or fictitious customs values.

flashcards.front: What broad problem do customs valuation rules try to prevent?
flashcards.back: They prevent arbitrary, fictitious, or inappropriate customs values that can unfairly increase duties.

flashcards.front: Why is customs value important when duty is ad valorem?
flashcards.back: Because the customs value is the base on which the percentage duty is calculated.

flashcards.front: Why are exporters concerned about unfair customs valuation?
flashcards.back: Because inflated customs values can undermine negotiated tariff reductions and destroy market access benefits.

flashcards.front: What can happen if customs uses inflated values when applying tariffs?
flashcards.back: Duties can be doubled or tripled unfairly, making imports more expensive than justified.

flashcards.front: What is the general objective of customs management and regulation under the GATT in relation to valuation?
flashcards.back: To ensure customs operations do not unnecessarily hinder international trade or offset concessions exchanged between WTO Members.

flashcards.front: What balance does the GATT framework try to maintain in customs valuation?
flashcards.back: It balances facilitation of trade with customs authorities’ need to ensure security, product safety, legal compliance, and fraud control.

flashcards.front: Which GATT article deals specifically with valuation for customs purposes?
flashcards.back: Article VII.

flashcards.front: Which GATT article deals with freedom of transit?
flashcards.back: Article V.

flashcards.front: Which GATT article deals with fees and formalities connected with importation and exportation?
flashcards.back: Article VIII.

flashcards.front: Which GATT article deals with marks of origin?
flashcards.back: Article IX.

flashcards.front: Which GATT article deals with publication and administration of trade regulations?
flashcards.back: Article X.

flashcards.front: What did GATT 1947 say customs value should not be based on?
flashcards.back: It should not be based on the value of merchandise of national origin or on arbitrary or fictitious values.

flashcards.front: What was meant by 'actual value' under GATT 1947?
flashcards.back: The price of the imported merchandise, or like merchandise, in sales in the ordinary course of trade under fully competitive conditions.

flashcards.front: Why was customs valuation addressed in the Tokyo Round?
flashcards.back: Because countries used different customs valuation systems and the original GATT rules lacked precision, creating unjustified barriers to trade.

flashcards.front: What was adopted in November 1979 regarding customs valuation?
flashcards.back: A Protocol to the Agreement on Implementation of Article VII.

flashcards.front: What was one major weakness of the Tokyo Round Valuation Code before the Uruguay Round?
flashcards.back: Less than one-third of the GATT contracting parties had signed it.

flashcards.front: What was the aim of the Uruguay Round negotiations on customs valuation?
flashcards.back: To improve, clarify, or expand the Tokyo Round Code and make it a multilateral agreement binding on WTO Members.

flashcards.front: What key agreement resulted from the Uruguay Round in 1994 on customs valuation?
flashcards.back: The Agreement on the Implementation of Article VII of GATT 1994, known as the Customs Valuation Agreement.

flashcards.front: What other instruments form part of the current customs valuation rules besides the Customs Valuation Agreement itself?
flashcards.back: Ministerial decisions and Committee decisions, including the Decision on Reasonable Doubts, the Decision on Minimum Values and Sole Agents, and G/VAL/5.

flashcards.front: What does Part I of the Customs Valuation Agreement cover?
flashcards.back: Rules on customs valuation in Articles 1 to 17.

flashcards.front: What does Part II of the Customs Valuation Agreement cover?
flashcards.back: Administration, consultations, and dispute settlement in Articles 18 and 19.

flashcards.front: What does Part III of the Customs Valuation Agreement cover?
flashcards.back: Special and differential treatment in Article 20.

flashcards.front: What does Part IV of the Customs Valuation Agreement cover?
flashcards.back: Final provisions in Articles 21 to 24.

flashcards.front: What valuation method has primacy under the Customs Valuation Agreement?
flashcards.back: The transaction value method.

flashcards.front: What are the core general principles in the preamble of the Customs Valuation Agreement?
flashcards.back: Primacy of transaction value, uniformity in valuation, fairness and neutrality, simple and equitable criteria, and consistency with commercial practice.

flashcards.front: Which articles contain the valuation methods in the Customs Valuation Agreement?
flashcards.back: Articles 1 to 8.

flashcards.front: Which article deals with currency conversion?
flashcards.back: Article 9.

flashcards.front: Which article deals with confidentiality?
flashcards.back: Article 10.

flashcards.front: Which article provides for a right of appeal?
flashcards.back: Article 11.

flashcards.front: Which article deals with publication?
flashcards.back: Article 12.

flashcards.front: Which article deals with release of goods?
flashcards.back: Article 13.

flashcards.front: Which article contains definitions?
flashcards.back: Article 15.

flashcards.front: Which article gives the importer the right to a written explanation?
flashcards.back: Article 16.

flashcards.front: What does Annex I to the Customs Valuation Agreement contain?
flashcards.back: Interpretative Notes.

flashcards.front: What does Annex II contain?
flashcards.back: The Technical WCO Committee provisions.

flashcards.front: What does Annex III contain?
flashcards.back: Provisions relating to developing countries.

flashcards.front: What does Article 14 say about the annexes?
flashcards.back: That the annexes are an integral part of the Customs Valuation Agreement.

flashcards.front: What two committees are established under Article 18?
flashcards.back: The WTO Valuation Committee in Geneva and the Technical Committee on Customs Valuation at the WCO in Brussels.

flashcards.front: What does the WTO Valuation Committee mainly handle?
flashcards.back: Policy issues and implementation issues.

flashcards.front: What does the Technical Committee on Customs Valuation do?
flashcards.back: It operates under the WCO in Brussels and supports technical interpretation and administration of customs valuation matters.

flashcards.front: Why does Article 20 provide special and differential treatment?
flashcards.back: Because developing country Members might face difficulties implementing all provisions of the Agreement when the WTO was created.

flashcards.front: Which article deals with reservations?
flashcards.back: Article 21.

flashcards.front: Which article deals with obligations regarding national legislation?
flashcards.back: Article 22.

flashcards.front: Which article provides for review of implementation and operation of the Agreement?
flashcards.back: Article 23.

flashcards.front: Which article deals with responsibilities of the WTO and WCO Secretariats?
flashcards.back: Article 24.

flashcards.front: Why are rules needed to preserve the value of tariff bindings?
flashcards.back: Because ad valorem duties depend on the customs value, and inflated values can nullify negotiated tariff concessions.

flashcards.front: How many authorized customs valuation methods are recognized under the Agreement?
flashcards.back: Six.

flashcards.front: What is Method 1 under the Customs Valuation Agreement?
flashcards.back: Transaction value.

flashcards.front: What is Method 2 under the Customs Valuation Agreement?
flashcards.back: Transaction value of identical goods.

flashcards.front: What is Method 3 under the Customs Valuation Agreement?
flashcards.back: Transaction value of similar goods.

flashcards.front: What is Method 4 under the Customs Valuation Agreement?
flashcards.back: Deductive value.

flashcards.front: What is Method 5 under the Customs Valuation Agreement?
flashcards.back: Computed value.

flashcards.front: What is Method 6 under the Customs Valuation Agreement?
flashcards.back: The fall-back method.

flashcards.front: Must the order of valuation methods be followed?
flashcards.back: Yes, the order must be followed.

flashcards.front: Can customs value be based on the selling price of domestically produced goods?
flashcards.back: No. That is a prohibited valuation method under Article 7.

flashcards.front: Can customs value be based on the higher of two alternative values?
flashcards.back: No. That is prohibited.

flashcards.front: Can customs value be based on the domestic market price of the country of exportation?
flashcards.back: No. That is prohibited.

flashcards.front: Can customs value be based on cost of production other than computed value?
flashcards.back: No. That is prohibited.

flashcards.front: Can customs value be based on the price of goods for export to a country other than the country of importation?
flashcards.back: No. That is prohibited.

flashcards.front: Are minimum customs values allowed under the WTO Customs Valuation Agreement?
flashcards.back: No. Minimum customs values are prohibited.

flashcards.front: Are arbitrary and fictitious values allowed under the Agreement?
flashcards.back: No. They are expressly prohibited.

flashcards.front: What may customs do if it has reason to doubt the truth or accuracy of the declared value?
flashcards.back: It may ask the importer for additional information or documentation.

flashcards.front: What must customs do if it doubts the declared value?
flashcards.back: It must inform the importer of the reasons for its doubts.

flashcards.front: What procedural fairness must customs provide where there are reasonable doubts?
flashcards.back: Customs must give the importer a reasonable opportunity to respond.

flashcards.front: How must customs communicate its final decision where it rejects the declared value?
flashcards.back: It must inform the importer in writing.

flashcards.front: Can transaction value be rejected if customs has unresolved reasonable doubts?
flashcards.back: Yes, transaction value can be rejected after the required process is followed.

flashcards.front: What is the basic rule in Article 1.1 on transaction value?
flashcards.back: The customs value of imported goods shall be the transaction value, subject to the Agreement’s conditions.

flashcards.front: What four core elements make up transaction value under Article 1.1?
flashcards.back: Price actually paid or payable, for the goods, when sold for export, plus permitted adjustments.

flashcards.front: What does 'price actually paid or payable' mean in customs valuation?
flashcards.back: It means whatever amount the buyer agrees to pay the seller to obtain the goods.

flashcards.front: Can customs reject the declared price just because it is lower than prevailing market prices?
flashcards.back: No. That alone is not a valid reason to reject transaction value.

flashcards.front: Can customs reject the declared price just because it is lower than prices for identical goods in other transactions?
flashcards.back: No. That alone is not enough to reject transaction value.

flashcards.front: Can a discounted sale still qualify for transaction value?
flashcards.back: Yes. A cash discount, volume discount, trade discount, or purchase discount does not automatically disqualify the value.

flashcards.front: What kinds of payments are included in the total payment for transaction value purposes?
flashcards.back: All payments the buyer makes to, or for the benefit of, the seller, whether directly or indirectly, including payments required as conditions of sale.

flashcards.front: What may often exist in addition to the price of the goods under Article 8?
flashcards.back: Payments or costs that are additional to and apart from the stated price of the goods.

flashcards.front: Name one payment that may be excluded from customs value calculation under the Agreement if conditions are met.
flashcards.back: Interest payments.

flashcards.front: Name another payment that may be excluded from customs value calculation.
flashcards.back: Dividend payments.

flashcards.front: Name one category of post-sale item that may be excluded from transaction value.
flashcards.back: Post-importation services and costs.

flashcards.front: Can advertising and marketing costs be excluded from customs value in appropriate cases?
flashcards.back: Yes, advertising and marketing costs may be excluded in appropriate circumstances.

flashcards.front: What is the core requirement for a sale to qualify as 'when sold for export'?
flashcards.back: The sale must be one for export to the country of importation.

flashcards.front: Must the seller be located in the exporting country for transaction value to apply?
flashcards.back: No. The seller can be located in any country, including the importing country.

flashcards.front: What kind of transfer must the sale involve for it to qualify under transaction value?
flashcards.back: It must involve an international transfer of goods.

flashcards.front: When can Article 8 adjustments be added to the price?
flashcards.back: Only if the charge is not already included in the price of the goods.

flashcards.front: On what kind of data must Article 8 additions be based?
flashcards.back: Objective and quantifiable data.

flashcards.front: Can customs make an Article 8 adjustment if it cannot quantify the relevant cost?
flashcards.back: No. If customs cannot quantify the cost, the adjustment cannot be made.

flashcards.front: Can customs add items other than those expressly listed in Article 8?
flashcards.back: No. Only the additions listed in Article 8 may be used.

flashcards.front: Name one type of addition listed in Article 8.
flashcards.back: Commissions and brokerage fees.

flashcards.front: Name another type of addition listed in Article 8.
flashcards.back: Containers and packaging.

flashcards.front: What are 'assists' under Article 8 in general terms?
flashcards.back: They are certain goods or services supplied by the buyer for use in producing the imported goods, which may need to be added if not already included.

flashcards.front: Are royalties and licence fees among the possible Article 8 additions?
flashcards.back: Yes.

flashcards.front: Are proceeds of subsequent resale among the Article 8 additions?
flashcards.back: Yes, where the relevant conditions apply.

flashcards.front: Can international transport costs be an Article 8 addition?
flashcards.back: Yes.

flashcards.front: Why does the relationship between buyer and seller matter in customs valuation?
flashcards.back: Because where companies are related, the relationship may or may not have influenced the price.

flashcards.front: Does the mere fact that buyer and seller are related automatically justify rejection of transaction value?
flashcards.back: No. Relationship alone is not in itself grounds for rejecting transaction value.

flashcards.front: What is the main question customs must ask when buyer and seller are related?
flashcards.back: Whether the relationship influenced the price.

flashcards.front: What are the two broad ways customs may review related-party transactions?
flashcards.back: By observing the circumstances surrounding the sale or by measuring against test values.

flashcards.front: Under Article 15, are members of the same family considered related parties?
flashcards.back: Yes.

flashcards.front: Are legally recognized partners in business considered related parties?
flashcards.back: Yes.

flashcards.front: Are buyer and seller related if one is the employer of the other?
flashcards.back: Yes.

flashcards.front: Are officers or directors of one another’s businesses related parties?
flashcards.back: Yes.

flashcards.front: What voting stock threshold helps establish relatedness through common ownership or control?
flashcards.back: Five percent or more of the outstanding voting stock of both buyer and seller held by a third person.

flashcards.front: Can buyer and seller be related if the buyer directly or indirectly controls the seller or vice versa?
flashcards.back: Yes.

flashcards.front: Can buyer and seller be related if both are controlled by a third person?
flashcards.back: Yes.

flashcards.front: Can buyer and seller be related if they together control a third person?
flashcards.back: Yes.

flashcards.front: What should customs consider first when examining the circumstances of sale between related parties?
flashcards.back: How the buyer and seller organize their commercial relations.

flashcards.front: What should customs consider second when examining the circumstances of sale?
flashcards.back: How the price in question was derived.

flashcards.front: What is one proof that the relationship did not influence the price?
flashcards.back: The price is consistent with normal industry practice.

flashcards.front: What is another proof that the relationship did not influence the price?
flashcards.back: The price is the same as in sales to unrelated parties.

flashcards.front: What is a third proof that the relationship did not influence the price?
flashcards.back: The price is enough to allow recovery of costs plus an average profit.

flashcards.front: What is the key question when using test values in related-party valuation?
flashcards.back: Whether the test values closely approximate the import price.

flashcards.front: Can transaction value be used if there is no sale?
flashcards.back: No.

flashcards.front: Can transaction value be used if there are restrictions on the disposition or use of the goods under Article 1.1(a)?
flashcards.back: No, subject to the conditions in the Agreement.

flashcards.front: Can transaction value be used if there are conditions for which a value cannot be determined at the time of import under Article 1.1(b)?
flashcards.back: No.

flashcards.front: Can transaction value be used if part of the proceeds of subsequent resale revert directly or indirectly to the seller under Article 1.1(c)?
flashcards.back: No, unless the Agreement’s requirements are satisfied through adjustment mechanisms where applicable.

flashcards.front: Can transaction value be used in related-party sales under Article 1.1(d)?
flashcards.back: Yes, but only if the relationship did not influence the price.

flashcards.front: What method comes immediately after transaction value if Method 1 cannot be used?
flashcards.back: Transaction value of identical goods under Article 2.

flashcards.front: What method comes after transaction value of identical goods?
flashcards.back: Transaction value of similar goods under Article 3.

flashcards.front: What method comes after transaction value of similar goods?
flashcards.back: Deductive value under Article 5.

flashcards.front: What method comes after deductive value?
flashcards.back: Computed value under Article 6.

flashcards.front: What is the last method in the hierarchy?
flashcards.back: The fall-back method under Article 7.

flashcards.front: If customs cannot use transaction value, must it move down the hierarchy method by method?
flashcards.back: Yes, the order must be followed.

flashcards.front: What is the baseline commitment for acceding WTO Members regarding customs valuation?
flashcards.back: They must implement the WTO customs valuation provisions in full from the date of accession.

flashcards.front: What prohibited valuation methods have WTO Members been especially vigilant in eliminating during accession?
flashcards.back: Minimum values and any system of reference prices or fixed valuation schedules used instead of transaction value.

flashcards.front: Why do notification requirements exist under Article 22.1?
flashcards.back: To provide transparency to the WTO Committee on each Member’s implementation of the Customs Valuation Agreement.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of notifying customs valuation legislation to the Committee?
flashcards.back: To provide the title, details, and relevant text of a Member’s national customs valuation legislation.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of the Checklist of Issues notification?
flashcards.back: It serves as the basis for an initial examination of a Member’s national legislation by the Committee.

flashcards.front: What are the two decisions mentioned as requiring notifications relating to interpretation of the Agreement?
flashcards.back: The Decision on the Treatment of Interest Charges and the Decision on Carrier Media.

flashcards.front: What must a Member notify if it applies the Decision on the Treatment of Interest Charges?
flashcards.back: The date from which it implemented the decision.

flashcards.front: What must a Member notify regarding the Decision on Carrier Media?
flashcards.back: Whether it applies paragraph 2 of the Decision.

flashcards.front: What is the basic recap formula for transaction value given in the presentation?
flashcards.back: Transaction value equals the price paid or payable, for the goods, when sold for export, plus only the adjustments permitted by Article 8.
